content.explanation:
Typing component props is the most fundamental skill when using TypeScript with React. Props define the contract between a parent component and its child, and TypeScript enforces that contract at compile time. The most common approach is to define a props interface and annotate the component parameter, giving you autocomplete, refactoring support, and compile-time error detection.

There are two main ways to type a React function component. The first is the explicit `React.FC<Props>` (or `React.FunctionComponent<Props>`) generic type, which automatically includes the `children` prop in older React types. The second and now more recommended approach is to simply type the props parameter directly: `function MyComponent(props: MyProps)`. The direct typing approach is preferred in modern React because it gives you more control over whether `children` is accepted and avoids some subtle issues with FC's implicit children and return type.

The `children` prop is typed as `React.ReactNode`, which covers strings, numbers, JSX elements, arrays, fragments, portals, null, and undefined - essentially anything React can render. When using the direct typing approach, you must explicitly add `children` to your props interface. You can also use the utility type `React.PropsWithChildren<Props>` to add a typed `children` prop to any interface.

Optional props are defined with the `?` modifier, and default values can be provided via destructuring defaults in the function parameter. Required props without `?` will cause a compile error if omitted by the parent. This makes it impossible to forget a required prop, catching bugs at development time rather than in production.

codeExamples.title: Basic Props Interface
codeExamples.output:
<button class="primary" disabled={false}>Submit</button>
<button class="danger" disabled={true}>Cancel</button>
<button class="primary" disabled={false}>Save</button>
codeExamples.explanation: Props with '?' are optional. Default values are provided via destructuring. TypeScript ensures the parent passes all required props and that values match the expected types.

codeExamples.title: Children Prop and PropsWithChildren
codeExamples.explanation:
Children can be typed as React.ReactNode (accepting anything renderable) or more narrowly. PropsWithChildren

adds an optional children prop to any props type.

codeExamples.title: Discriminated Union Props
codeExamples.explanation: Discriminated union props let you model components where different prop combinations are valid depending on a discriminant field. TypeScript narrows the type inside each case.

notes: Prefer direct props typing over React.FC - it gives you explicit control over children and avoids implicit return type issues.
Use discriminated union props when a component has mutually exclusive prop combinations based on a 'type' or 'variant' field.
Always define props as an interface or type alias - inline object types in the function parameter are harder to export and reuse.
commonPitfalls: Using React.FC in modern React 18+ projects where children is no longer implicitly included in FC - you must add children explicitly to your props.
Defining default values with defaultProps instead of destructuring defaults - defaultProps is deprecated for function components.

exercises.title: Component Props Interface
exercises.description: Create a typed UserCard component that accepts name, email, role (optional, defaults to 'user'), and isActive (boolean). Return a formatted string representation.
hints: Use '?' after the property name to make role optional, and provide a default value in the destructuring pattern.
Use a ternary expression to convert the boolean isActive into the string 'active' or 'inactive'.

exercises.title: Discriminated Union Props
exercises.description: Create a Notification component that uses discriminated union props. The component accepts different props based on a 'kind' discriminant.
exercises.expectedOutput:
INFO: Update available
WARNING: Disk space low (dismissible: true)
ERROR [503]: Connection failed
hints: Use a union of object types with 'kind' as the common discriminant field.
In a switch statement on props.kind, TypeScript narrows the type so you can access properties specific to each variant.

content.explanation:
React's useState hook is a generic function that can infer the state type from its initial value or accept an explicit type parameter. When you write `useState(0)`, TypeScript infers the state type as `number`. When you write `useState<string | null>(null)`, you explicitly tell TypeScript that the state can be either a string or null. Explicit typing is essential when the initial value does not represent all possible future values of the state.

Event handling in React with TypeScript requires understanding the synthetic event system. React wraps native browser events in `SyntheticEvent` objects, and TypeScript provides specific generic event types for each: `React.MouseEvent`, `React.ChangeEvent`, `React.FormEvent`, `React.KeyboardEvent`, and more. Each event type is generic over the HTML element that triggered it, such as `React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>` or `React.MouseEvent<HTMLButtonElement>`.

Form events are particularly important in React applications. The `onChange` handler for input elements uses `React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>`, giving you typed access to `event.target.value`. The `onSubmit` handler for form elements uses `React.FormEvent<HTMLFormElement>`, and you typically call `event.preventDefault()` to prevent page reload. When typing event handler functions separately from JSX, use `React.EventHandler<E>` or the specific handler type like `React.ChangeEventHandler<HTMLInputElement>`.

A common pattern is to type state as a union that includes the initial state and all possible future states. For example, data fetching state might be typed as `{ status: 'idle' } | { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'success'; data: T } | { status: 'error'; error: string }`. This discriminated union pattern ensures you handle all possible states and cannot accidentally access `data` when the status is 'loading'.

codeExamples.title: useState with Explicit Types
codeExamples.explanation: useState infers the type from the initial value. Use explicit generics when the initial value (like null) does not represent all possible states.

codeExamples.title: Typing Event Handlers
codeExamples.output:
Input changed: email = john@test.com
Clicked at (150, 200)
codeExamples.explanation: React event types are generic over the element type. ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement> gives you typed access to target.value and target.name.

codeExamples.title: Discriminated Union State Pattern
codeExamples.output:
Ready to fetch
Loading...
Data: ["item1","item2"]
Error: Network timeout
codeExamples.explanation: The discriminated union pattern ensures exhaustive handling of all async states. TypeScript narrows the type in each case branch, so 'data' is only accessible when status is 'success'.

notes: Always provide an explicit type parameter to useState when the initial value is null or undefined - otherwise TypeScript infers the state type as 'null' or 'undefined' only.
Use React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement> for text inputs, React.ChangeEvent<HTMLSelectElement> for selects, and React.FormEvent<HTMLFormElement> for form submissions.
The discriminated union state pattern is excellent for modeling loading/error/success states in data fetching hooks.
commonPitfalls: Using useState(null) without a type parameter - TypeScript infers the type as 'null' and you cannot set it to any other value later.
Typing event handlers with the native DOM event types instead of React's synthetic event types - React events have different signatures than native events.

exercises.title: Typed State Manager
exercises.description: Create a simple typed state manager that tracks form fields. Implement get and set operations with type safety.
hints: Use the spread operator to merge partial updates into the current state: { ...this.state, ...updates }.
Store a copy of the initial state in the constructor so reset() can restore it.

exercises.title: Async State Machine
exercises.description: Implement a typed async state machine using discriminated unions. Model idle, loading, success, and error states with proper type narrowing.
exercises.expectedOutput:
State: idle - Waiting for action
State: loading - Fetching data...
State: success - Got 3 items: a, b, c
State: error - Timeout
hints: Use 'status' as the discriminant field in your union type - each variant has a unique status string.
In the switch statement, TypeScript narrows the type so you can access 'data' only in the 'success' branch.

content.explanation:
React's useRef hook serves two distinct purposes, and each requires different typing in TypeScript. When used to reference a DOM element, you pass null as the initial value and type it as `useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null)`. This creates a `RefObject` with a readonly `current` property. When used as a mutable instance variable (to store values that persist across renders without triggering re-renders), you include null in the type union or provide a non-null initial value, creating a `MutableRefObject`.

The key difference between `RefObject<T>` and `MutableRefObject<T>` is that RefObject's `current` is readonly - you cannot reassign it because React manages the DOM reference. MutableRefObject's `current` is read-write, allowing you to store and update values freely. TypeScript determines which one you get based on whether null is part of the generic type parameter or just the initial value.

React's createContext is a generic function that requires a default value. In TypeScript, the type of the context is inferred from the default value, or you can specify it explicitly: `createContext<ThemeContext | undefined>(undefined)`. The challenge is that if the default is undefined, every consumer must check for undefined before using the context. A common pattern is to create a custom hook that throws an error if the context is undefined, eliminating the need for null checks at every consumption point.

Ref forwarding with TypeScript uses `React.forwardRef<RefType, PropsType>`, where the first generic parameter is the type of the forwarded ref and the second is the props type. This is essential for library components that need to expose their underlying DOM element to parent components. The function passed to forwardRef receives props and ref as separate arguments, and the ref is typed as `React.ForwardedRef<RefType>`.

codeExamples.title: useRef for DOM Elements and Mutable Values
codeExamples.output:
DOM ref initial: null
Render count: 3
Timer ID: 42
Timer cleared: null
codeExamples.explanation: DOM refs use RefObject with readonly current. Mutable refs (for counters, timer IDs, etc.) use MutableRefObject where current is writable.

codeExamples.title: Typed Context with Custom Hook
codeExamples.output:
Current theme: dark
Theme toggled
Theme is dark: true
codeExamples.explanation: A custom hook that throws when context is undefined eliminates null checks at every consumption point. Consumers get a guaranteed non-undefined context value.

codeExamples.title: Generic Context Factory
codeExamples.output:
User: Alice
Logged in: true
Counter: 42
codeExamples.explanation: A generic context factory creates type-safe contexts with built-in undefined checks. This pattern reduces boilerplate when creating multiple contexts.

notes: Use useRef<HTMLElement>(null) for DOM refs (readonly current) and useRef<T>(initialValue) with a non-null initial for mutable refs (writable current).
Create a custom hook (e.g., useTheme) that throws when context is undefined - this eliminates null checks at every consumer.
The generic context factory pattern is a powerful way to reduce context boilerplate while maintaining full type safety.
commonPitfalls: Typing useRef<HTMLDivElement | null>(null) instead of useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null) - the former creates a MutableRefObject where you can accidentally set current to null, while the latter creates a ReadonlyRefObject managed by React.

Using createContext without a default value or with an incorrect type - TypeScript infers the context type from the default, so an undefined default without explicit generics gives you 'undefined' type.

exercises.title: Mutable Ref Tracker
exercises.description: Create a class that simulates useRef behavior for tracking previous values. Implement a PreviousValueTracker that stores the current and previous values.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Current: 1, Previous: null
Current: 5, Previous: 1
Current: 10, Previous: 5
hints: In the update method, save the current value to previous before overwriting current with the new value.
Initialize previous as null in the constructor since there is no previous value at creation time.

exercises.title: Type-Safe Context Store
exercises.description: Build a generic context store that supports multiple named contexts, each with its own type. Implement register, get, and list operations.
exercises.expectedOutput:
theme, auth, locale
dark
Alice
en-US
hints: Use a Map<string, any> internally. The generic type parameter on get<T> is used for casting the return value.
Throw an Error in get() if the name is not found in the store.

content.explanation:
Custom hooks are the primary mechanism for reusing stateful logic in React, and typing them correctly in TypeScript is crucial for a good developer experience. A custom hook is simply a function whose name starts with 'use', and it can call other hooks. The return type of a custom hook determines what the consumer gets - it can be a single value, an object, a tuple, or even a generic type.

The most common return type patterns for custom hooks are tuples and objects. Tuples like `[value, setValue]` follow the useState convention and allow destructuring with custom names. However, TypeScript infers arrays, not tuples, by default. You must use `as const` or explicitly type the return as `[Type1, Type2]` to get a tuple type. Objects like `{ data, loading, error }` are better when the hook returns many values, as they are self-documenting and order-independent.

Generic custom hooks accept type parameters that flow through to their return types, creating highly reusable logic. For example, a `useLocalStorage<T>` hook can store any type of value while preserving type safety. The generic parameter constrains both the initial value and the returned state. You can also add constraints to generics, like `<T extends string | number>`, to limit what types can be used with the hook.

Advanced patterns include overloaded hook signatures (where the return type changes based on the arguments), conditional return types, and hooks that return discriminated unions. These patterns are common in production codebases and are frequent interview topics. Understanding how to type a hook that returns different shapes based on its configuration options demonstrates deep TypeScript proficiency.

codeExamples.title: Tuple vs Object Return Types
codeExamples.output:
isOpen: false
Count: 10
After increment: 11
After increment: 12
After decrement: 11
After reset: 10
codeExamples.explanation: Tuple returns must be explicitly typed to prevent TypeScript from inferring a union array. Object returns are better for hooks with many return values.

codeExamples.title: Generic Custom Hook
codeExamples.explanation: The generic type T flows through the entire hook - the initial value, getter return type, and setter parameter are all constrained to T.

codeExamples.title: Hook with Discriminated Union Return
codeExamples.output:
Users: Alice, Bob
Error: Network error
Idle status: idle
codeExamples.explanation: Returning a discriminated union forces consumers to check the status before accessing data or error, preventing runtime null errors.

notes: Always explicitly type tuple returns as [Type1, Type2] - without this, TypeScript infers a union array (Type1 | Type2)[] which loses positional type information.
Generic hooks should constrain their type parameter when the hook only works with certain types, e.g., <T extends Record<string, any>>.
Use discriminated union return types for hooks that have distinct states (loading, success, error) to force exhaustive state handling.
commonPitfalls: Returning [value, setter] without an explicit tuple type - TypeScript infers (boolean | (() => void))[] instead of [boolean, () => void], making both elements of union type.
Creating generic hooks without constraints - a useStorage<T> hook that serializes to JSON will fail at runtime with functions or symbols, so constrain T appropriately.
interviewTips: Be ready to explain the difference between returning a tuple [value, setter] and an object { value, setter } from a custom hook - tuples allow renaming, objects are self-documenting.
Know how to type a generic useFetch<T> hook that returns a discriminated union with loading, success, and error states.
Understand why 'as const' is needed for tuple returns and how TypeScript's type inference differs between arrays and tuples.
Practice writing a custom hook that composes multiple React hooks (useState, useEffect, useCallback) with correct types.

exercises.title: Generic useList Hook
exercises.description: Create a generic useList<T> hook that manages a list of items with add, remove, and clear operations. Return the operations as an object.
exercises.expectedOutput:
apple, banana, cherry
Size: 3
apple, cherry
Size: 2
hints: Use a closure variable to hold the list array. Return methods that mutate this array.
The remove method should use splice(index, 1) to remove a single item at the given index.

exercises.title: Typed useReducer-like Hook
exercises.description: Create a typed state machine hook that accepts a reducer function and initial state, similar to useReducer. Support dispatching typed actions.
hints: The useReducer function takes a reducer function and initial state, returning the current state and a dispatch function.
The reducer must handle all action types via a switch statement. TypeScript will narrow the action type in each case branch.

content.explanation:
Generic React components are components that accept a type parameter, allowing them to work with different data types while maintaining full type safety. This is one of the most powerful patterns in TypeScript React development. A generic List component, for example, can render a list of any item type while keeping the onSelect callback fully typed to that specific item type.

To create a generic component, you define the component function with a type parameter: `function List<T>(props: ListProps<T>)`. The type parameter T flows through the props interface, connecting the items array type to the renderItem function type to the onSelect callback type. When the component is used, TypeScript infers T from the items prop, so consumers do not need to specify the generic type explicitly in most cases.

Constrained generics add requirements to the type parameter using the `extends` keyword. For example, `<T extends { id: string }>` ensures that whatever type is used must have an `id` property. This is essential for components that need to access certain properties on the items - like using `item.id` as a React key. Without the constraint, TypeScript would not allow accessing `.id` on the generic type.

Generic components are particularly useful for building reusable UI libraries. Common examples include Select/Dropdown components (generic over the option type), Table components (generic over the row data type), and Form components (generic over the form values type). The key insight is that the generic type connects related props - the data source, the render function, and the event callbacks all share the same type, ensuring consistency.

codeExamples.title: Generic List Component
codeExamples.explanation: T is inferred from the items array. The renderItem and onSelect callbacks automatically know the exact type of each item.

codeExamples.title: Constrained Generic Component
codeExamples.output:
Select:
  - United States (US)
  - United Kingdom (UK) [selected]
  - Japan (JP)
codeExamples.explanation: The 'extends HasId' constraint ensures T always has an 'id' property. This allows the component to use item.id for comparisons while keeping the full type for callbacks.

codeExamples.title: Generic Table Component
codeExamples.output:
Product | Price | Available
--- | --- | ---
Laptop | $999 | Yes
Phone | $699 | No
codeExamples.explanation: The Column interface uses 'keyof T' to ensure column keys correspond to actual properties of the data type. Custom render functions receive the correctly typed value.

notes: TypeScript infers the generic type from the items/data prop in most cases - consumers rarely need to specify the type parameter explicitly.
Use 'extends' constraints when your component needs to access specific properties on the generic type, like 'id' for keys.
The 'keyof T' type in column definitions ensures that column keys are valid property names of the data type, preventing typos.
commonPitfalls: Defining generic components with React.FC - the FC type does not support generics directly. Use regular function declarations with explicit type parameters instead.
Forgetting to constrain the generic type - without 'extends HasId', you cannot access .id on the generic type, even if all consumers pass objects with id.

exercises.title: Generic Dropdown
exercises.description: Create a generic Dropdown function that works with any item type that has an 'id' and 'label' property. Implement render and selection logic.
hints: The constraint 'T extends Labelable' ensures every item has id and label properties.
Use map to transform each item into a string with a [x] or [ ] prefix based on whether its id matches selectedId.

exercises.title: Generic DataGrid with Sorting
exercises.description: Build a generic DataGrid function that renders tabular data and supports sorting by a specified column key.
exercises.expectedOutput:
name | age | dept
Charlie | 35 | Eng
Alice | 30 | Eng
Bob | 25 | Design
hints: Use the spread operator [...data] to create a copy before sorting, so the original array is not mutated.
Use keyof T to type the columns array and sortBy parameter, ensuring only valid property names are accepted.
